description:
You are acting as an in-house penetration tester who has been given an *offline* mirror of three web pages.  
Every page is stored as a simple, self-contained HTML file in  

/home/user/webmirror/

Your objectives are:

1. Automatically visit (i.e. “open” in a headless way) every page listed in the text file  
   /home/user/webmirror/targets.lst  
   NOTE: each line in that file is just the file-name (no path).  

2. For each page, detect the following two client-side issues:  
   a) Any <script> element whose **src** attribute begins with the insecure scheme **“http://”** (case-insensitive).  
   b) Any use of JavaScript’s **eval()** function anywhere in the page (inline or inside an external script).  

3. Create a directory called  

   /home/user/vulnscan/  

   if it does not yet exist, and write the single file  

   /home/user/vulnscan/vulnerability_report.json  

4. The JSON MUST follow **exactly** this pretty-printed structure so that automated tests can validate it:

{
    "site1.html": {
        "insecure_script_src": [
            "http://example.com/foo.js",
            "http://cdn.example.com/bar.js"
        ],
        "uses_eval": true
    },
    "site2.html": {
        "insecure_script_src": [],
        "uses_eval": false
    }
    ... one object entry per HTML file ...
}

Formatting rules the grader will enforce:

• 4-space indentation.  
• Each HTML file listed in targets.lst becomes a top-level key (just the file-name, **no** leading path).  
• The array under "insecure_script_src" must preserve the order the offending <script src=> tags appear in the original HTML.  
• "uses_eval" is a JSON boolean (true / false, **not** a string).  
• Keys are ordered **alphabetically** in the resulting JSON file.  

5. When everything is done, print the absolute path of vulnerability_report.json to stdout so the assessor can cat it quickly if needed.

Hints you are free to ignore:
• Popular ways to drive a headless browser include `python -m http.server` + `selenium` with a headless Chrome/Chromium or even simpler, parsing the files directly with Python + BeautifulSoup if a full browser is over-kill.  
• No root privileges are available, so stay in user-writable areas.  

Success criteria:

• /home/user/vulnscan/vulnerability_report.json exists.  
• Its JSON content and formatting is exactly as specified above and reflects the real vulnerabilities in the three supplied HTML files.  
• Directory/file permissions must allow read access for the current user.  
• Path to the report is echoed at the end of the run.
